content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Learn how modern applications distribute millions of requests across multiple servers without slowing down or crashing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A website suddenly goes viral.
Within minutes, hundreds of thousands of people start opening it at the same time.
Some are logging in.
Others are placing orders.
Thousands of API requests arrive every second.
Yet somehow, the website keeps working.
No errors.
No crashes.
No noticeable slowdown.
So what’s stopping a single server from getting overwhelmed?
The answer isn’t a faster CPU or more RAM.
It’s something that sits quietly in front of your application, making thousands of routing decisions every second.
That component is called a Load Balancer.
If you’ve ever wondered how companies like Amazon, Netflix, Google, or Spotify handle millions of users simultaneously, understanding load balancers is one of the best places to start.
Why Every Backend Developer Should Understand Load Balancers
